steps.label: Definition 7.1.1
steps.subtitle: Diagonalizable matrices
steps.bodyHtml:
Consider a linear transformation $T(\vec{x})=A\vec{x}$ from $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. Then A (or T) is said to be diagonalizable if the matrix B of T with respect to some basis is diagonal
By Theorem 3.4.4 and Definition 3.4.5, matrix A is diagonalizable if and only if A is similar to some diagonal matrix B, meaning that there exists an invertible matrix S such that $S^{-1}AS=B$ is diagonal
To diagonalize a square matrix A means to find an invertible matrix S and a diagonal matrix B such that $S^{-1}AS=B$.

steps.label: Definition 7.2.2
steps.subtitle: Eigenvectors, eigenvalues, and eigenbases
steps.bodyHtml:
Consider a linear transformation $T(\vec{x})=A \vec{x}$ from $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{n}$
A nonzero vector $\vec{v}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ is called an eigenvector of A (or T) if $$A \vec{v}=\lambda \vec{v}$$ for some scalar $\lambda$. This $\lambda$ is called the eigenvalue associated with eigenvector $\vec{v}$.
A basis $\vec{v_1},\cdots,\vec{v_n}$ of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ is called an eigenbasis for A (or T) if the vectors $\vec{v_1},\cdots,\\vec{v_n}$ are eigenvectors of A.

steps.label: Theorem 7.1.3
steps.subtitle: Eigenbases and diagonalization
steps.bodyHtml:
The matrix A is diagonalizable if and only if there exists an eigenbasis for A. If $\vec{v_1},\cdots,\vec{v_n}$ is an eigenbasis of A, with $A \vec{v_1}=\lambda_1 \vec{v_1},\cdots,A \vec{v_n}=\lambda_n \vec{v_n}$, then the matrices
           $$S=[v_1\dots v_n]$$ and 
           $$B=diag(\lambda_1\dots\lambda_n)$$ will diagonalize A, meaning that 
           $$S^{-1}AS=B$$
           Conversely, if the matrices S and B diagonalize A, then the column vectors of S will form an eigenbasis for A, and the diagonal entries of B will be the associated eigenvalues.

steps.label: Theorem 7.1.4
steps.bodyHtml: The possible real eingenvalues of an orthogonal matrix are 1 and -1.

steps.label: Summary 7.1.5
steps.subtitle: Characterizations of invertible matrices
steps.bodyHtml:
For an nxn matrix A, the following statements are equivalent:
i. A is invetible
ii. The linear system $A \vec{x}=\vec{b}$ has a unique solution $\vec{x}$, for all $\vec{b}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.
iii. $rref(A)=I_n$
iv. rank(A)=n
v. im(A)=$\mathbb{R}^{n}$
vi. ker(A)=${\vec{0}}$
vii. The column vectors of A form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$
viii. The column vectors of A span $\mathbb{R}^{n}$
ix. The column vectors of A are linearly independent.
x. det(A)$\neq 0$
xi. 0 fails to be an eigenvalue of A.
